reason: Con18 challenges the premise that higher wages will simply reduce teen hours by suggesting employers may replace workers with automation
Con argues firms may outsource, threatening the premise that teens will retain jobs to work fewer hours.
Con claims outsourcing reduces American jobs, undermining the premise that teen employment will persist.
Con says a wage hike is only a bandage, suggesting the pro conclusion about teen study time does not follow.
Con asserts higher wages force firms to cut staff, undermining the premise that teens can keep jobs and work fewer hours.
Con cites a post‑2009 drop in teen employment, directly challenging the premise that teens will stay employed.
Con presents evidence of reduced teen employment after a wage hike, undermining the pro premise.
The outsourcing claim challenges the premise that higher wages will keep teens in school by suggesting jobs may disappear.
It attacks the premise that higher wages protect teen employment by pointing to job loss through off‑shoring.
It argues that a wage hike is merely a superficial fix, so the conclusion that it reduces teen dropout does not follow.
It challenges the premise that higher wages allow firms to retain or hire teens by asserting firms can’t afford as many employees.
Empirical evidence of a sharp drop in teen employment after a wage hike undermines the premise that higher wages keep teens in school.
The claim that the 2009 increase reduced teenage employment directly attacks the premise linking higher wages to lower dropout rates.
Con18 suggests employers may replace workers with kiosks, undermining the premise that raising the wage will benefit workers
Con14 argues higher wages could trigger outsourcing, undermining the premise that workers will benefit from a federal raise
B argues that a wage hike is merely a bandage, suggesting A’s conclusion that it will benefit workers does not follow.
B claims higher wages reduce hiring ability, challenging the inference that raising the federal minimum wage will benefit workers.
B provides evidence of a sharp teen employment drop after a wage increase, undermining A’s benefit claim.
B asserts the 2009 wage hike significantly reduced teenage employment, directly challenging A’s conclusion of benefit.
Pro4 shows wages are low relative to productivity, undermining con18’s premise that paying a clerk a high wage is a realistic concern.
By asserting wages lag productivity, pro4 challenges con14’s premise that companies would need to outsource because wages are high.
Pro asserts productivity outpaces wage increases implying no harm, while con reports a sharp drop in teenage employment, a direct contradiction.
Pro’s claim of outpacing productivity conflicts with con’s evidence of reduced teenage employment after the wage hike.
B challenges the premise that economic growth can absorb wage hikes by claiming higher wages limit hiring
B provides empirical evidence that a wage increase reduced teenage employment, undermining A's premise that growth outpaced wage rises
B asserts the 2009 wage hike cut teenage jobs, attacking the premise that growth makes wage hikes harmless
con70 predicts price increases that directly conflict with pro36's claim of little or no price rise
con70b asserts widespread price pass‑through, contradicting pro36's empirical finding of no price increase
Con14 argues that outsourcing could occur despite GDP growth, showing the pro’s conclusion that raises are harmless doesn’t follow even if the premise is true.
Con14b claims outsourcing reduces American jobs, challenging the inference that minimum‑wage hikes are economically benign.
Con70 predicts full cost pass‑through to consumers, showing the pro’s claim that raises are harmless is not logically guaranteed.
Con70b extends the cost‑pass‑through argument to all firms, similarly undercutting the pro’s conclusion.
Con12 argues higher wages limit hiring, challenging the pro’s inference that economic growth proves no negative employment effects.
Con76 presents evidence of a sharp teenage‑employment drop after a wage hike, questioning the pro’s claim that raises are without adverse effects.
Con76b asserts the 2009 increase reduced teenage employment, undermining the pro’s implication that raises are benign.
B provides evidence that raising the minimum wage led to a sharp drop in teenage employment, challenging the premise that increasing the wage is beneficial.
B provides evidence that a past minimum‑wage increase hurt employment, challenging the premise that raising the wage is harmless.
B argues automation can replace clerks, attacking the premise that higher wages are a viable solution.
B suggests firms may outsource to cheaper labor, undermining the premise that a higher domestic minimum wage will keep jobs at home.
B claims outsourcing reduces American jobs, attacking the premise that raising the wage will not harm domestic employment.
B attacks the political motive, questioning the premise that the wage argument is based on objective economic need.
B dismisses public understanding, undermining the premise that popular support reflects a sound economic argument.
B argues a wage hike is only a superficial fix, showing the conclusion that higher wages solve the problem does not follow even if the buying‑power premise is true.
B claims firms will pass the full cost to consumers, attacking the premise that a higher wage is without broader economic harm.
B extends the cost‑pass‑through argument to other firms, undermining the premise that raising the wage has limited side effects.
B states higher wages limit hiring capacity, attacking the premise that businesses can absorb wage increases without reducing employment.
B shows a negative employment effect of a past minimum‑wage hike, challenging the premise that raising the wage (as A implies) is beneficial.
B claims the 2009 increase cut teenage jobs, undermining the assumption that a higher wage (as A suggests) would be a positive policy.
A claims firms can outsource to cheaper labor, which weakens B's premise that firms must pass increased labor costs onto consumers
Con70’s premise that firms can pass 100% of costs implies they can afford wages, which challenges con12’s premise that firms cannot afford to hire.
Con76’s observed employment drop undermines con70’s premise that firms will simply pass costs to consumers without cutting jobs.
Evidence of reduced teenage employment contradicts con70’s assumption that firms can fully shift labor costs to prices.
Con70b’s claim that firms can pass costs suggests they can afford wages, opposing con12’s premise of unaffordable hiring.
The employment decline reported in con76 challenges con70b’s premise that firms will merely pass costs to consumers.
Con76b’s claim of reduced teenage employment undermines con70b’s assumption of full cost pass‑through without job loss.
[symmetric rebuttal] Pro asserts productivity outpaces wage increases implying no harm, while con reports a sharp drop in teenage employment, a direct contradiction.
[symmetric rebuttal] Pro’s claim of outpacing productivity conflicts with con’s evidence of reduced teenage employment after the wage hike.
[symmetric rebuttal] con70 predicts price increases that directly conflict with pro36's claim of little or no price rise
[symmetric rebuttal] con70b asserts widespread price pass‑through, contradicting pro36's empirical finding of no price increase
